coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic gaseous formaldehyde degradation using N-CQDs/OVs-TiO2 composite under visible light: Unraveling the synergistic effects of N-CQDs and oxygen vacancies
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yang
He, Xiaoqing
Gao, Peng
Feng, Li
Zhang, Liqiu
coredata.dc:description: Limited utilization of photogenerated charge carriers in titanium dioxide under visible light have hinder its application development. To address this challenge, a novel N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and oxygen vacancies (OVs) synergistically decorated on TiO2 (P25) was synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Under visible light irradiation, the first order reaction rate constants of formaldehyde (HCHO) photocatalytic oxidation by OVs-TiO2 and N-CQDs/OVs-TiO2 was significantly higher than that of pristine P25, with 10.1 and 16.7 folds increase, respectively. Characterization results confirmed the generation of OVs on the surface of N-CQDs/TiO2 composite. The optical and electrochemical experiments suggested the electron capture center effect of OVs and the properties of N-CQDs in unique up-converted photoluminescence, efficient charge separation, as well as significant adsorption in visible light region. In addition, the work function also clarified that photoelectrons could transfer from N-CQDs to OVs-TiO2. Furthermore, different relative humidity and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments demonstrated that the hydroxyl radical (•OH) was the dominant reactive radical in HCHO photodegradation. The •O2 − could also enhance the photodegradation efficiency of HCHO. This work provides an in-depth understanding on the complementary roles of N-CQDs and OVs and is helpful for designing metallic oxide photocatalysts for volatile organic compounds removal.
coredata.dcterms:subject: HCHO
N-CQDs
Oxygen vacancies
Up-converted photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic performance for gaseous HCHO degradation
Mechanism of photocatalytic HCHO by N-CQDs/OVs-TiO2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( BLX202256 ), the National Key Research and Development of China ( 2023YFC3207700 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52170021 and 52370028 ), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2023M730274 ), the Beijing Municipal Education Commission through the Innovative Transdisciplinary Program Ecological Restoration Engineering.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectra (a), FTIR spectrum (b) and XRD patterns (c) of N-CQDs and CQDs.
XPS spectra of N-CQDs for Survey (a), N 1s (b), C 1s (c).
HRTEM images of NCOT-5 under 20 nm (a), 10 nm (b), and 5 nm (c–d).
XRD patterns (a) and FTIR spectra (b) of P25, OT and NCOT-5, Raman spectra (c) of OT and NCOT-5, OVs-EPR spectra (d) of P25 and NCOT-5.
XPS survey spectra (a), high resolution XPS spectra of O1s for P25, OT and NCOT-5 (b), C1s (c), N 1s (d) for NCOT-5.

Visible light photodegradation of HCHO over OT with different hydrothermal time (a) and corresponding kobs (b), Visible light photodegradation of HCHO over NCOT with different amount of N-CQDs (c) and corresponding kobs (d), Visible-NIR light photodegradation of HCHO over NCOT with different amount of N-CQDs (e) and the comparison of degradation efficiency by NCOT between visible light (420–780 nm) and visible-NIR light (λ > 420 nm) (f).
HCHO conversion efficiency under different relative humidity (a) and oxygen concentrations (at minimum humidity conditions of 5 % for reactor) (b).
Cycling experiments of HCHO photodegradation efficiency by OT and NCOT-5 (a), the concentrations of HCHO and CO2 in the photocatalytic process (b).
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a), Transient photocurrent response (b), and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (c) of P25, OT and NCOT-5.
Calculated work function of (a) OVs-(101) TiO2, (b) N-CQDs/OVs-TiO2. Charge density difference and corresponding charge transfer (c–e) on N-CQDs/OVs-TiO2。.
Band gap energy plots (a) of P25, OT, NCOT-5, the VB-XPS spectrum of OT (b), DMPO-radicals signals of EPR (c–d) for NCOT-5 under dark and visible light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of proposed mechanism in NCOT photocatalytic process.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic gaseous formaldehyde degradation using N-CQDs/OVs-TiO2 composite under visible light: Unraveling the synergistic effects of N-CQDs and oxygen vacancies

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Water Pollution Source Control & Eco-remediation
College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Limited utilization of photogenerated charge carriers in titanium dioxide under visible light have hinder its application development. To address this challenge, a novel N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and oxygen vacancies (OVs) synergistically decorated on TiO2 (P25) was synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Under visible light irradiation, the first order reaction rate constants of formaldehyde (HCHO) photocatalytic oxidation by OVs-TiO2 and N-CQDs/OVs-TiO2 was significantly higher than that of pristine P25, with 10.1 and 16.7 folds increase, respectively. Characterization results confirmed the generation of OVs on the surface of N-CQDs/TiO2 composite. The optical and electrochemical experiments suggested the electron capture center effect of OVs and the properties of N-CQDs in unique up-converted photoluminescence, efficient charge separation, as well as significant adsorption in visible light region. In addition, the work function also clarified that photoelectrons could transfer from N-CQDs to OVs-TiO2. Furthermore, different relative humidity and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments demonstrated that the hydroxyl radical (•OH) was the dominant reactive radical in HCHO photodegradation. The •O2 − could also enhance the photodegradation efficiency of HCHO. This work provides an in-depth understanding on the complementary roles of N-CQDs and OVs and is helpful for designing metallic oxide photocatalysts for volatile organic compounds removal.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Formaldehyde (HCHO) is a poisonous and noxious gas that has a strong stimulating odor. Long-term exposure to HCHO may lead to serious health issues (Zhang et al., 2021). Therefore, it is essential to control indoor HCHO emissions by enhancing ventilation and effectively removing HCHO from indoor air (Yuan et al., 2019). To decompose HCHO molecules, numerous robust technologies have been developed, including adsorption, thermal catalytic oxidation, photocatalysis and others (Ye et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2019). However, both adsorption and thermal catalytic oxidation are limited by their high energy consumption and low sustainability. Among these methods, photocatalysis is considered to be the most promising approach due to its high-efficiency, energy savings and lack of secondary pollution (Zhou et al., 2021b).

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is widely used in photocatalysis because of its excellent properties, such as chemical stability, nontoxicity and high photoactivity (Djokić et al., 2020). The main crystal forms of TiO2 include anatase, rutile, and brookite. Degussa P25, a frequently-used TiO2 crystal form with a composition of 75 % anatase and 25 % rutile, exhibits strong photocatalytic activity under UV irradiation (λ < 380 nm) due to its wide band gap (∼3.2 eV) (Sendão et al., 2022). However, the proportion of UV light in solar spectrum is less than 5.0 % (Zeng et al., 2015). Therefore, it is essential to enhance the photocatalytic performance of P25 under the visible light irradiation.

Recently, various modified strategies have been conducted to improve the visible light photocatalytic activity of P25, including metal doping, heterojunction formation, and defects engineering (Zhou et al., 2023). Among these strategies, oxygen vacancies (OVs) engineering is a convenient method for enhancing visible light response of P25, which has attracted increasing attention. Knotek and Feibelman (1978) firstly discovered that high-energy electrons and ions can preferentially desorb O2− ions from TiO2 surfaces through an “Interatomic Auger Recombination” route. It can be inferred that the separation efficiency of charge carriers in OVs-TiO2 is also significantly improved. After that, Li et al. (2020) prepared a OVs-TiO2 in hydrogen (H2) atmosphere and found that OVs can induce the separation of photogenerated charge carriers in TiO2 under visible light. This is primarily because OVs in TiO2 form energy level that can trap the photoelectrons from valence band (VB), leading to a significant increase in spatial charge carriers separation efficiency (Cheng et al., 2020). It can be seen that OVs not only enhance the visible light response of catalyst but also improve the electron withdrawing properties, enhancing spatial charge carriers separation efficiency. Nevertheless, OVs in crystal structure can also form a new “recombination center”, leading to charge carriers recombination and reducing photocatalytic activity. Therefore, it is vital to design a novel cocatalyst for further trapping charge carriers on the surface of OVs-TiO2.

N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are a novel nanocarbon material that have attracted increasing attention due to their unique up-conversion photoluminescence properties, excellent photoelectric characteristics, and good biocompatibility (Tian et al., 2015; Ou et al., 2021). By doping N atom into CQDs, the photoelectron transfer efficiency can be significantly enhanced, the work function of materials can be tuned, and the charge delocalization can be induced (Ji et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2023). Furthermore, N-CQDs can form π-conjugated heterojunction structures with semconductor, thereby accelerating charge carriers transfer. Wang et al. (2017) successfully synthesized N-CQDs/g-C3N4 using a facile polymerized method, and the photocatalytic indomethacin degradation efficiency was remarkably increased to be 135.1 % under visible light irradiation. Yu et al. (2020) separately prepared a N-CQDs/OVs-BiOBr composites using a hydrothermal method. Notably, the photocatalytic degradation performance of N-CQDs/OVs-BiOBr for tetracycline increased to be 10.1 × 10−4 min−1 in water under full-spectrum light irradiation. Previous studies have confirmed that N-CQDs act as a cocatalyst, improving charge carriers transfer and extending light adsorption. However, to the best of our knowledge, N-CQDs modified OVs-P25 has never been reported in gaseous formaldehyde degradation. Additionally, the charge carrier transfer mechanism at N-CQDs and OVs in P25 interface remains unclear. Therefore, further insights into the synergistic effect of N-CQDs and OVs for improving gaseous formaldehyde degradation efficiency are worth studying.

Herein, we designed and prepared a series of N-CODs/OVs-TiO2 composites using a hydrothermal method for efficient gaseous HCHO photodegradation. The main objectives of this study were threefold: (i) Evaluate the activity of N-CODs/OVs-TiO2 composites under different relative humidity and oxygen concentration conditions; (ii) Investigate the synergistic effects of N-CODs and OVs in TiO2 by density functional theory (DFT) calculations and experiments at the atomic level; (iii) Explore the mechanism of charge carriers transfer and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation in the process of gaseous HCHO photodegradation. These findings could lead to the development of high-activity photocatalysts for gaseous pollutant degradation, providing a practical solution for environmental remediation and air quality improvement.

All the reagents were of analytical purity. The 2-hydroxy-1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid triammonium salt (99 %) was purchased from Energy Chemical. The ethylenediamine (99 %) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The citric acid monohydrate was purchased from HUSHI in Shanghai. HCl was purchased from Modern Oriental Technology Development in Beijing. The Degussa P25 (TiO2) was purchased from King Chemical.
The preparation methods and characterizations of photocatalysts were detailed in Supporting Information.

All the spin-polarized DFT calculations were conducted using the Vienna Ab-inito Simulation Package (VASP) (Kresse G. and Furthmüller J. 1996). The electron-ion interactions were described by the Projected Augmented-Wave (PAW) potentials, while the exchange-correlation interactions were calculated by employing the Perdew-Burke-Enzerhof (PBE) functional of Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA) (Perdew et al., 1996) with a Hubbard U extension (U value) of 2.0 eV for Ti. The van der Waals interaction was described using vdW-D3 method developed by Grimme (Grimme et al., 2010). The plane-wave energy cutoff was set as 400 eV. The convergence threshold was set as 1.0 × 10−5 eV in energy and 0.02 eV per Angstrom in force. A vacuum layer of 20 Å was adopted for slab models to avoid the periodic interactions and the bottom two layers was fixed during relaxation. The Brillouin zone was modeled using a gamma-centered Monkhorst-Pack grid, with a 2×3×1 and 4×2×1 grid adopted for OVs-(101)TiO2 and N-CQDs/OVs-(101)TiO2.

Fig. 1 a showed the optical properties of N-CQDs and CQDs by the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Obviously, both N-CQDs and CQDs exhibited strong absorption in the range of 200–300 nm and the absorption spectrum of N-CQDs increased intensely in the range of 300–400 nm. Two intrinsic peaks at 240 nm and 350 nm occurred in the spectrum of N-CQDs, corresponding to π-π* electronic transitions of the aromatic sp2 and n-π* transition of CO bond, respectively (Zhu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2016). The FTIR was employed to explore the difference of functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs and CQDs in Fig. 1b. The representative spectra peaks of the main oxygen-containing organic functional groups existed on the surface CQDs and N-CQDs, including CO and -C-O-C- located at 1534 cm−1 and 1044 cm−1, respectively. Notably, several peaks at 1457 cm−1 (-NH), 1282 cm−1 (C–N), 1144 cm−1 (-C–NH–C-), and 3244 cm−1 (C–OH and N–H) appeared on the surface of N-CQDs (Li et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2021a). However, there was noise level peak on the surface of CQDs near the above positions, confirming the successful doping of nitrogen on the surface of CQDs. In addition, the XRD was also employed to characterize the crystal structure of CQDs and N-CQDs in Fig. 1c. A sharp peak was located at 27° in N-CQDs spectrum, corresponding to the typical (002) diffraction of crystalline graphitic carbon (Zhou et al., 2007). However, the pattern of CQDs showed a broadening peak around 24.5°, belonging to a low crystalline structure (Wang et al., 2018). N-CQDs with high crystalline structure were more favorable to construct the interface contract. Subsequently, HRTEM images (Fig. S1) showed that N-CQDs presented a quasi-spherical morphology. The lattice spacing of N-CQDs was 0.32 nm corresponding to (002) crystal planes of graphite phase (Zhang et al., 2016), and the graphite phase match well with the XRD results. Besides, the chemical composition of N-CQDs was shown in XPS spectra. Fig. 2 a showed that the elements of C, N and O existed on the surface of N-CQDs. The contents of C, N, and O elements were estimated as 61.5 %, 17.14 % and 21.36 %, respectively. Fig. 2b exhibited the high resolution of XPS for N1s, the peak located at 399.4 eV was assigned to C–N–C and the other peak near 400.7 eV could be corresponded to N–C3 bonds (Edison et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). In addition, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 1c) showed three peaks of 284.1 eV, 285.6 eV and 287.3 eV, belonging to CC, C–N, and N–CN, respectively (Wang et al., 2015; Zeng et al., 2015). It can be seen that XPS results confirmed the successful N doping in CQDs. Notably, many chemical functional groups (such as amino, carboxyl) existed on the surface of N-CQDs and facilitated the binding of N-CQDs to photocatalysts through chemical adsorption (Gao et al., 2021a).

Finally, the up-converted photoluminescence property of N-CQDs was probed by PL spectrum. As shown in Fig. 3 , the up-converted emissions were remarkably centered at 456 nm under different excitation wavelength from 550 to 800 nm. The PL results suggested that the long wavelength light could converted to short visible light with more powerful energy by N-CQDs. Herein, N-CQDs, as a cocatalyst, could significantly enhance the efficiency of the utilization in visible light. The migration of photogenerated charge carriers would be improved in N-CQDs based photocatalysts.

The characterization results of OT were consistent with the our previous study (Wang et al., 2022). As a benchmark, pristine P25 delivered a noise level peak in EPR spectrum. In contrast, the peak near g = 2.003 was observed in all OT samples, corresponding to OVs (Fig. S2). The EPR signal intensity of OT increased gradually at the g factor of 2.003 when the hydrothermal time increased from 4 h to 5 h, indicating the content of OVs increased with the extension of hydrothermal time (Gao et al., 2021b). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S3, FTIR spectra further confirmed the accumulation of hydroxyl groups (-OH) on the surface of OT. The adsorption quantity of these –OH groups decreased as the hydrothermal time increased to 6 h. Besides, compared to P25, the corresponding OVs peaks ratio of OT-4h, OT-5h and OT-6h increased from 6.1 at% to 15.4 at.% (Fig. S4) (Miao et al., 2020). Obviously, the OT-5h exhibited the highest OVs contents (15.4 at.%), which is consistent with FTIR results. This can be attributed to the fact that OVs facilitate the dissociation of water molecules into two –OH groups (Jung et al., 2010). According to the peak ratio of OVs to all oxygen peaks, it could be estimated that the contents of OVs were 0 at.%, 6.1 at.%, 15.4 at.% and 8.3 at.% in P25, OT-4h, OT-5h and OT-6h, respectively (Fig. S4). Overall, the peak intensity showed a volcano-type trend toward the hydrothermal time, and the OT-5h sample showed the highest OVs content.

The internal structure of NCOT-5 was analyzed by HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 4 a–b, N-CQDs particles were distributed on the surface of OT evenly. According to Fig. 4c–d, the crystalline structure of OT with lattice spacing of 0.351 nm corresponded to (101) lattice plane (Junkaew et al., 2021), and N-CQDs have the lattice about 0.332 nm corresponding to (002) crystal planes of graphite phase, which was consistent with previous results. Then, the phase structures of P25, OT and NCOT-5 were characterized by XRD. Fig. 5 a showed that the diffraction peaks of all samples could be attributed to the anatase-rutile structure. It was worth noting that the (110) (101) (111) (204) (221) phase corresponding to rutile structure showed a slight increase in OT and NCOT, which was due to the existence of oxygen vacancies in the composites. In addition, no evident change of the crystalline structure could be observed in the XRD pattern of NCOT-5, suggesting the low content and high dispersion of N-CQDs in the samples (Di et al., 2015). FTIR spectra analysis further confirmed the formation of NCOT-5 composites. As shown in Fig. 5b, the peaks located at 3215 cm−1 belonged to C–OH and N–H, the peaks located at 1562 cm−1, 1432 cm−1, 1284 cm−1 and 1136 cm−1 corresponded to –NH, C–N, and -C–NH–C-, respectively (Wang et al., 2017). The carboxyl, carbonyl and amino were observed obviously in N-CQDs and NCOT, confirming the successful doping of N-CQDs on the surface of OT. Raman spectroscopy was used to analyze the variation of surface structure between OT and NCOT-5. As shown in Fig. 5c, the Raman spectral line of NCOT-5 drifted up, clarifying the photoluminescence of NCOT-5 was intensively. Simultaneously, two peaks corresponded to D band and G band appeared at 1350 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1, indicating the carbon atoms existed on the surface of OT (Han et al., 2014). This result further confirmed the successful doping of N-CQDs on OT. Moreover, EPR experiment was carried out to confirm the existence of OVs in NCOT-5. There was a negligible background noise value in P25 (Fig. 5d). In contrast, it was worth noting that the value near g = 2.003 increased obviously in NCOT-5, confirming the existence of OVs and N-CQDs in NCOT-5 (Zhang et al., 2018).

The XPS survey spectrum was employed to provide further structural insight in NCOT-5. The full-scale XPS spectrum could be seen in Fig. 6 a, and four elements Ti, O, C, and N were detected in NCOT-5 composite. In the O 1s region of the spectrum (Fig. 6b), the OT and NCOT-5 samples could be divided into three peaks of 529.6, 531.8, and 533 eV belonging to lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancies and surface hydroxyl, respectively (Yang et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2021a). In Fig. 6c, no OVs and surface hydroxyl peaks could be found in P25. However, the OVs contents in OT and NCOT-5 were 4.9 at.% and 15.4 at.%, respectively. In the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 6c), the peak in 284.4 eV was ascribed to CC, and the 285.3 eV, and 288 eV were attributed to C–N, and N–CN, respectively. In the XPS of N1s (Fig. 6d), the peak located at 399.4 eV was assigned to the C–N–C bond and another peak at 400.7eV was corresponded to N–C3 in the N-CQDs. Notably, compared to the XPS of N-CQDs (Fig. 2a), the C, N, O elements also can be detected in the XPS survey of NCOT-5, suggesting successful doping of N-CQDs in composites. Overall, the XPS results demonstrated the introduction of oxygen vacancies in TiO2 and the successful introduction of N-CQDs into OT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance for gaseous HCHO degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the photocatalytic performance of OT for HCHO degradation under visible light irradiation was measured. The error of each experiment was controlled within 5.0 %. Before the light illumination, the adsorption of HCHO on the surface of photocatalysts was performed to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. As shown in Fig. 7 a, OT-5h showed the highest photocatalytic performance compared to OT-4h and OT-6h. This is related to the abundant OVs in OT-5h. In sharp contrast, the degradation efficiency of HCHO by pristine P25 was only 9.0 % under visible light. The first order kinetic constants of HCHO photodegradation by OT were displayed in Fig. 7b. As expected, OT-5h had the highest kinetic constant (kobs) of 0.01111 min−1. Combined with previous characterization results in OT, it can be concluded that the OVs content of TiO2 was positively to the performance of OT. The ameliorated performance OT could be ascribed to the captured center role of OVs. Subsequent discussions in photocatalytic enhancement mechanism (section 3.3) supported this result. Hence, the OT-5h sample was used to carry out following experiments. Subsequently, the wide-spectrum photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared NCOT composites were evaluated under visible light and vis-NIR-light irradiation. The photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of HCHO by NCOT-3, NCOT-5 and NCOT-7 were 59.4 %, 71 % and 58.9 % under visible light, respectively (Fig. 7c). The corresponding kobs of HCHO photodegradation were shown in Fig. 7d. Obviously, NCOT-5 has the highest kobs of 0.01842 min−1, which is 16.7 times higher than that of pristine P25. The photocatalytic oxidation experiments were further proceeded under vis-NIR light. As shown in Fig. 7e, the photodegradation efficiency of HCHO ranked in the order of NCOT-5>NCOT-3>NCOT-7>OT > P25, which were 68 %, 56 %, 53 %, 52 %, and 20 %, respectively. Compared with the corresponding photodegradation efficiency under visible light, the value exhibited a slight decrease in NCOT (Fig. 7f). This reason can be explained that NCOT can not be excited under NIR light irradiation. The mechanisms of the enhanced photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs under visible light were summarized in unique up-converted photoluminescence, efficient charge separation, as well as significant adsorption under visible light.

To further investigate the influence of gas environment in HCHO photodegradation, a series of degradation experiments under different relative humidity conditions were deliberately carried out. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the HCHO conversion efficiency increased from 53 % to 85 % when RH increased from 5.0 % to 40 %. The results confirmed the fact that H2O could be oxidized by NCOT to generate •OH (demonstrated in section 3.4) and •OH could participate in the process of HCHO oxidation. However, the HCHO conversion efficiency decreased obviously when RH increased from 40 % to 80 %, which could be ascribed to the competitive adsorption of moisture and HCHO on the surface of NCOT. Moreover, the HCHO conversion experiment was carried out under different oxygen concentration (Fig. 8b). The conversion efficiency of HCHO showed a slightly upgrade with the increased concentration of oxygen. Hence, it could be deduced that O2 in humid air also played an important role in HCHO photodegradation process.

The stability and reusability of NCOT and OT nanocomposite was studied by cyclic photocatalytic degradation HCHO in Fig. 9 a. The photodegradation efficiency of NCOT-5 in 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th and 5th were 74 %, 63 %, 48 %, 43 % and 35 %, respectively. However, the photodegradation efficiency for HCHO of OT in 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th and 5th were 48 %, 27 %, 22 %, 20 %, and 19 %, respectively. Obviously, the stability of NCOT was ameliorated compared to OT in HCHO removal. This is attributed to that the effective interface contact between OT and N-CQDs could expose more active sites for efficient HCHO photodegradation, resulting the relative high stability. So, the corresponding CO2 concentration was measured and presented in Fig. 9b. As can be observed, the theoretical ratio of CO2 to the decomposed HCHO was approximately 1:1 in the actual photocatalytic process. This indicates that HCHO was effectively oxidized to CO2 in NCOT-5 photocatalytic process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic HCHO by N-CQDs/OVs-TiO2

ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the enhancement mechanism of photocatalysts can be summarized as: (1) the capacity of light adsorption and (2) the separation efficiency of spatial charge carriers. For the first point, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of NCOT-5, OT and P25 were recorded (Fig. 10 a). Pristine P25 showed no absorption above the wavelength of 400 nm, while the absorption of OT in visible light region of 420–780 nm increased obviously. In sharp contrast, the NCOT-5 showed a large red shift in the visible light region, suggesting the strongest optical absorption capacity (Ji et al., 2018). The enhancement of visible light absorption in OT could be attributed to the existence of OVs. In addition, N-CQDs served as a photosensitizer has the strong visible light absorption ability, further ameliorated the ability of light absorption in OT. As consequence, the enhanced capacity of light absorption promoted the formation of photogenerated charge carriers.

For second point, transient photocurrent response was carried out deliberately to interrogate the separation efficiency of spatial charge carriers in P25, OT, and NCOT-5. Maintaining the same operational conditions, the photocurrent intensity was positively functional correlated with separation efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers (Zhao et al., 2021). As displayed in Fig. 10b, the current density of NCOT was 1.2 A/cm−2, that was higher than that of P25 (0.05 A/cm−2) and OT (0.2 A/cm−2) under visible light illumination, etc., suggesting the abridged recombination of photogenerated charge carriers by the existence of OVs. Notably, NCOT-5 exhibited considerably higher photocurrent intensity than P25 and OT, confirming the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carries could be further effectively ameliorated by the introduction of N-CQDs. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was further employed to explore the charge transport capability of P25, OT, and NCOT-5 under visible light irradiation (Fig. 10c). The radius of samples ranked in the order of P25 > OT > NCOT-5. Generally, the radius of EIS was negatively functional correlated with the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers (Gao et al., 2022). Therefore, the photogenerated charge carriers in NCOT-5 had the highest separation efficiency, confirming that the N-CQDs and OVs could accelerate the separation of spatial charge carriers and improve the photocatalytic performance manifestly. The aforementioned results could be explained as follows: the OVs in OT could form donor energy levels so that the photogenerated electrons from VB could be captured, thus preventing the electrons recombination with holes (Gao et al., 2021b). When the N-CQDs was available on the surface of OT, they acted as electron capture centers. The photogenerated electrons transferred to conduction band (CB) in OT were trapped by N-CQDs, thus improving the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers (Wang et al., 2023). Herein, the mechanisms of the enhanced photocatalytic performance of NCOT could be summarized as follows: (1) The absorption of visible light in NCOT was enhanced obviously by N-CQDs and OVs. (2) The photoelectrons in P25 could be captured by N-CQDs and OVs, and the separation efficiency of spatial charge carriers in P25 was improved significantly. (3) The effect of up-converted photoluminescence ability in N-CQDs could absorb long wavelength light and then emit short wavelength. Under the irradiation of short wavelength light with high energy, more ROS could be generated on NCOT, boosting the degradation efficiency of HCHO.

To rationally demonstrate the electron transfer mechanism in NCOT system, the work function of OT and NCOT was deliberately investigated by DFT calculation. As shown in Fig. 11 a-b, the work function of OT (101) and NCOT was estimated as follows: ɸ = Evac- Ef, where ɸ was the work function, Evac was the energy of a stationary electron at the vacuum level, and Ef was the Fermi levels (Low et al., 2019). Hence, the work functions of OT and NCOT were calculated to be 4.42 eV and 4.36 eV, respectively. Obviously, the work function of NCOT decreased slightly after the introduction of N-CODs to OT. This is suggested that electrons could transfer from N-CODs to OT (Gao et al., 2021a). Subsequently, the Ef of N-COT (−0.18 eV) was more positive than that of OT (−1.77 eV). That means when the N-CQDs got into contact with OT, the electrons tended transferred from the N-CQDs to the surface of OT to achieve Ef equilibrium. Herein, an internal electric field could be formed due to the charged region closed to the interface of NCOT. In this way, the charge carrier migration between OT and N-CQDs was accelerated significantly, thereby greatly improving the photocatalytic performance of NCOT. The migration direction of photoelectrons was consistent with the previous characterization results. Furthermore, the Bader charge analysis and the charge density differences provide more evidence of the strong interface interaction between N-CQDs and OT. This interaction effectively promotes charge carriers transfer, enhancing the photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 11c–e, the yellow area represents electrons enrichment, indicating a higher concentration of electrons, while the light blue area represents electrons deficiency, indicating a lower concentration of electrons. The calculated number of electrons transferred between N-CQDs and OT is 0.43 e, suggesting that a strong interface contact is formed between these two components. This interface contact plays a crucial role in facilitating charge carriers transfer and ROS generation during gaseous HCHO photodegradation.

As shown in Fig. 12 a, according to Kubelka-Munk function, the band gap energy (Eg) of P25, OT, and NCOT-5 were 3.20 eV, 2.80 eV, 1.63 eV, respectively (Yang et al., 2015). Compared with the band gap energy of P25, the OT showed a decreased in band gap energy. The narrower band gap can upgrade the separation efficiency of spatial charge carriers, thus promoting the photocatalytic performance of OT. Then, the VB positions of OT had been ascertained by the XPS valence spectra (Fig. 12b). The maximal values of VB in OT was determined to be 2.34 eV. According to the energy band calculation formula
E
C B
=
E
V B
−
E g
, the CB of OT was determined to be −0.46 eV. Subsequently, the major ROS in the process of HCHO photodegradation were scrutinized by EPR experiments. As shown in Fig. 12c–d, there were no •OH and •O2 − were detected under dark reaction conditions. However, the typical characteristic intensity peak of 1:2:2:1 corresponded to •OH and the weak sextet signal of •O2 − were detected. Generally, the energy of CB and VB determined the redox energy of photogenerated charge carriers (Di et al., 2015). The VB of OT (2.34 eV) was more positive than the redox potential of •OH/OH− (+1.99 eV vs. NHE) (Gao et al., 2022). Herein, h+ on the VB of OT could oxidize OH− to form •OH. Simultaneously, the valence state of CB was more negative than the redox potential of O2/•O2 − (−0.046 eV) (Gao et al., 2020), so the photogenerated electrons on CB reduced O2 to form •O2 − via reaction:
O 2
+
e −
→ ·
O 2 −
. The theoretical analysis reinforced the EPR experiment results. In accord with previous humidity and oxygen concentration experiment results, both •OH and •O2 − could participate in the process of HCHO photodegradation and •OH was the dominant active species. In addition, it was worth noting that the conversion of •O2 − to •OH would occur via
2 •
O 2 −
+ 2
H +
→ 2 • O H +
O 2
when oxygen existed in humid air. In the process of HCHO photodegradation, •OH plays the main reactive species participate in HCHO degradation.

HCHO degradation according to the procedure for HCHO photocatalytic oxidation by NCOT. And a possible mechanism of HCHO photodegradation by NCOT was shown in Fig. 13 . The enhanced photocatalytic performance over NCOT could be attributed to two causes. Firstly, due to the existence of oxygen vacancies in OT, a donor energy level was formed. The donor energy level could effectively suppress the photogenerated electrons falling back from CB into VB, then the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers was improved significantly. Secondly, the doping of N-CQDs on the surface of OT resulted in a distinct increase in the absorption of visible light, and the absorption capacity were significantly superior to pristine P25. Additionally, the electron capture center effect of N-CQDs promoted the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers. Specifically, the long emission wavelength could be converted to short visible light with more powerful energy by the up-converted photoluminescence effect of N-CQDs. Thus, a cascade of •OH could be generated. Based on above discussions, NCOT was excited to form more photogenerated charge carriers under visible light. In consequence, the main ROS including •OH and •O2 − generated under visible light irradiation engaged in the photodegradation of HCHO. The •OH was the dominant radical in HCHO photocatalytic oxidation, and •O2 − could also improve the photodegradation efficiency of HCHO.

In summary, NCOT composite crystals were successfully synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal method. Characterizations confirmed the formation of OVs in P25, the preparation of N-CQDs and the successful doping of N-CQDs on the surface of OT. The optical and electrochemical experiments clarified the up-converted photoluminescence properties, bandgap narrowing and electron trapping center effect of N-CQDs. Simultaneously, the synergism mechanism of the electron trapping effect between N-CQDs and OVs was also investigated systematically. Additionally, DFT calculations demonstrate photoelectrons could transfer between N-CQDs and OVs-TiO2. In comparison to pristine P25, the OT and NCOT exhibited high photocatalytic activity for HCHO under visible light. The kobs for HCHO photodegradation of OT and NCOT were 10.1 and 16.7 times higher than that of pristine P25. The •OH was the dominant ROS in HCHO photodegradation and •O2 − could compensate the consumed •OH in humid air. This research could provide more insights into the design of high efficiency photocatalysts by synergistic modification of metal oxides by N-CQDs and OVs.

Yang Wang: Writing – original draft. Xiaoqing He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Peng Gao: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Li Feng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Liqiu Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (BLX202256), the National Key Research and Development of China (2023YFC3207700), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52170021 and 52370028), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023M730274), the Beijing Municipal Education Commission through the Innovative Transdisciplinary Program Ecological Restoration Engineering.
